claims.claim: For LiNi0.9Co0.05Mn0.05O2 batteries (Sample A, 211 Ah, 342.06 Wh/kg), thermal runaway was triggered when the positive tab reached 119°C and the negative tab reached 123°C, with a critical temperature defined as a temperature change rate of 1°C/s.
claims.quote: The critical temperature for TR is defined as a temperature change rate of 1 °C/s. The temperature of the positive tab rose to 119 °C, and the negative tab rose to 123 °C.

claims.claim: Sample A (211 Ah, 342.06 Wh/kg) reached a peak chamber temperature of 557.5°C and a peak chamber pressure of 386.5 kPa during thermal runaway, while Sample B (256 Ah, 325.82 Wh/kg) reached a peak chamber temperature of 409.86°C and a peak chamber pressure of 330.5 kPa.
claims.quote: The highest temperature inside the chamber reached 557.5 °C, and the highest pressure inside the chamber reached 386.5 kPa. For Sample B... The highest temperature inside the chamber reached 409.86 °C, and the highest pressure inside the chamber was 330.5 kPa.

claims.claim: The higher-energy-density Sample A (342.06 Wh/kg) exhibited a mass loss rate of 73% during thermal runaway, while the lower-energy-density Sample B (325.82 Wh/kg) exhibited a mass loss rate of 64.87%, indicating that higher energy density leads to more severe mass loss.
claims.quote: Sample A showed a larger mass loss after TR, indicating a more severe TR phenomenon... The mass loss rates for A and B were 73% and 64.87%

claims.claim: The normalized gas production of Sample B (256 Ah) was 2.182 L/Ah compared to 1.958 L/Ah for Sample A (211 Ah) at standard conditions (0°C, 101 kPa), indicating that gas production increases with battery capacity.
claims.quote: the gas production is converted to standard conditions, resulting in 1.958 L/Ah for Sample A and 2.182 L/Ah for Sample B. It can be observed that gas production is related to the capacity of the battery.

claims.claim: The mixed vent gases from Sample A (211 Ah) had a lower flammable limit of 8.49% and a flammable range of 49.81%, while Sample B (256 Ah) had a lower flammable limit of 7.43% and a flammable range of 44.45%, indicating Sample B gas poses a higher ignition risk due to its lower LFL.
claims.quote: Table 6. The flammable limits. Sample UFL LFL Flammable Range A 58.3 8.49 49.81 B 51.88 7.43 44.45

claims.claim: The linearized gas flow rate during thermal runaway for the high-power 18650 cell (SonyVTC5A) is 22.15 gs−1 and for the high-energy cell (LGMJ1) is 27.92 gs−1.
claims.quote: Our results show, that the linearized gas flow rate for the high-power and high-energy cell is 22.15 gs−1 and 27.92 gs−1, respectively.

claims.claim: The peak gas velocity during thermal runaway for the high-energy 18650 cell (LGMJ1, NMC811) follows a single peak asymmetrical pattern with a maximum of 398.5 ms−1, while the high-power cell (SonyVTC5A, NCA) shows a bumpy pattern with a maximum of 260.9 ms−1.
claims.quote: in case of the high-energy cell, it follows a single peak asymmetrical pattern with a peak of 398.5 ms−1, while the high-power cell shows a bumpy pattern with a maximum gas velocity of 260.9 ms−1.

claims.claim: The transition time from safety valve opening to thermal runaway for the high-power NCA cell (SonyVTC5A) was 69.46 ± 12.67 s, whereas the non-reheated high-energy NMC811 cell (LGMJ1) required 189.54 ± 87.56 s, meaning the high-energy cell took at least twice as long.
claims.quote: It took 69.46 ± 12.67 s for the SonyVTC5A cells to go from opening of the safety valve into TR, whereas the non-reheated LGMJ1 cells needed 189.54 ± 87.56 s.

claims.claim: The averaged mass loss during the thermal runaway stage was 57.50% for SonyVTC5A and 63.45% for LGMJ1, as measured by the oscilloscope.
claims.quote: The boxplots (e) and (f) underneath show averaged mass losses with the Osci for SonyVTC5A and LGMJ1 of 57.50% and 63.45%, respectively.

claims.claim: The gas flow rate and gas velocity during venting can be determined from only the recoil force and weight force measurements, without requiring knowledge of vent pressure, vent temperature, vent area size, or ejecta composition.
claims.quote: With this approach, the size and stability of venting area, venting temperature and cell temperature, venting pressure and cell pressure, as well as the composition of the ejecta are irrelevant for deriving the gas velocity and gas flow rate.

claims.claim: The simulated gas flow rates from venting and thermal runaway propagation models for 18650 cells ranged from 2.5 gs−1 to 3.5 gs−1, which is one order of magnitude lower than the experimentally measured values of 22.15 gs−1 and 27.92 gs−1.
claims.quote: the simulated gas flow rates are given with 2.5 gs−1 [31] to 3.5 gs−1 [35] and one order of magnitude lower than our measured gas flow distribution with 22.15 ± 14.70 gs−1 for SonyVTC5A and 27.92 ± 9.500 gs−1 for LGMJ1 cells.

claims.claim: The expansion force of a 58 Ah Li(Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1)O2 prismatic battery reached a peak value of 1980 N at −13.5 s relative to voltage-zero, representing a 12-fold increase within a duration of 2.5 s starting from −15 s.
claims.quote: At the −15 s mark: Expansion force initiates a sharp ascent, reaching a peak value of 1980 N at −13.5 s, representing a 12-fold increase within a duration of 2.5 s.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of a 58 Ah Li(Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1)O2 battery, the temperature measured 1 cm above the safety release valve (TE) exhibited a maximum rate of temperature rise of 93 °C/s within the time frame of −13 s to −5 s relative to voltage-zero.
claims.quote: within the −13 s to −5 s timeframe, TE exhibits the highest rate of temperature rise, reaching 93 °C/s

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of a 58 Ah Li(Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1)O2 battery, the maximum momentary eruption velocity upon safety valve opening was 90 m/s, occurring at the 1.5-s mark, with solid particles jetting to heights of up to 3.4 m.
claims.quote: The most intense occurrence of the first mode transpires at the 1.5-s mark, with a momentary eruption velocity of 90 m/s upon the opening of the safety valve... the generated solid particles can jet to heights of up to 3.4 m.

claims.claim: Explosive phenomena during thermal runaway were initiated by ejected sparks igniting combustible gases expelled during the jetting process, with the explosion center located within 0.1 to 0.5 m from the safety valve.
claims.quote: explosive phenomena are initiated by the ignition of the ejected sparks, which ignite the combustible gases expelled during the jetting process... the explosion center to be within a range of 0.1 to 0.4 m from the safety valve.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of a 58 Ah Li(Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1)O2 battery, the mass loss rate was 32.4 ± 2%, the maximum flame propagation velocity exceeded 17 m/s, and the maximum flame area reached 0.79 m².
claims.quote: The mass loss rate (%) 32.4 ± 2, The maximum flame propagation velocity (m/s) >17, The maximum flame area (m2) 0.79

claims.claim: When ambient pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 20 kPa, peak HRR decreases from 1.2 kW to 0.65 kW for a single cylindrical 18650 cell, from 14.2 kW to 1.2 kW for 5 cylindrical cells, from 26.3 kW to 6.1 kW for a 10 Ah pouch cell, and from 41.1 kW to 8.7 kW for a 15 Ah pouch cell.
claims.quote: The peak HRR decreases from 1.2 kW to 0.65 kW, 14.2 kW to 1.2 kW, 26.3 kW to 6.1 kW, and 41.1 kW to 8.7 kW for 1 cylindrical cell, 5 cylindrical cells, a 10 Ah pouch cell, and a 15 Ah pouch cell, respectively.

claims.claim: When pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 20 kPa, the average TR trigger time is shortened for cylindrical cells (from 145 s to 133 s) but delayed for pouch cells (from 625 s to 643 s for 10 Ah, and from 730 s to 761 s for 15 Ah).
claims.quote: The average trigger time (t2) of spark ejections for cylindrical cells is 145 s in 95 kPa and 133 s in 20 kPa. However, t2 increases from 625 s to 643 s, and from 730 s to 761 s for 10 Ah and 15 Ah pouch cells, when the pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 20 kPa.

claims.claim: As pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 20 kPa, the average first venting time (safety valve opening or bag rupture) is shortened from 144 s to 133 s for 18650 cylindrical cells, from 475 s to 461 s for 10 Ah pouch cells, and from 559 s to 533 s for 15 Ah pouch cells.
claims.quote: As the pressure decreases from 95 kPa to 20 kPa, the average values of t1 (the average open time of the safety valve or the rupture time of the bag) decrease from 144 s to 133 s, 475 s to 461 s, and 559 s to 533 s, for 18650 cylindrical cells, 10 Ah pouch cells, and 15 Ah pouch cells, respectively.

claims.claim: Under 20 kPa, average peak CO concentrations are higher than under 95 kPa, increasing from 0.19 vol.% to 0.28 vol.% for single cylindrical cells, from 0.21 vol.% to 0.91 vol.% for 5 cylindrical cells, from 0.16 vol.% to 0.59 vol.% for 10 Ah pouch cells, and from 0.24 vol.% to 0.72 vol.% for 15 Ah pouch cells.
claims.quote: the average peak values of CO increase from 0.19 vol.% to 0.28 vol.%, 0.21 vol.% to 0.91 vol.%, 0.16 vol.% to 0.59 vol.%, and 0.24 vol.% to 0.72 vol.%, with the pressure drop from 95 kPa to 20 kPa.

claims.claim: Pouch cells have higher peak HRR and THR (higher thermal fire hazard) but lower THC and CO concentrations per unit capacity compared to cylindrical 18650 cells, both at 95 kPa and 20 kPa.
claims.quote: the pouch cell with a soft aluminum–polymer bag has higher thermal hazards of TR fire, but a lower concentration of toxic/combustible gases than cylindrical cells with hard cans. The cylindrical cell has a higher risk of toxicity and potential explosion than the pouch cell in the TR process.

claims.claim: At 20 kPa, no combustion flames occur during the entire TR process of a single cylindrical 18650 cell, because the oxygen mass concentration per volume is less than the stoichiometric ratio required for ignition.
claims.quote: at a pressure of 20 kPa, there are no combustion flames in the whole TR process. This is probably because large amounts of combustible gases and ejections, released rapidly after the sparks (safety valve opened completely), are not ignited under 20 kPa for the reason that oxygen mass concentration per volume is less than the stoichiometric ratio.

claims.claim: The CO/CO2 ratio under 20 kPa (2.05, 1.99, 1.87, and 2.55 for four test groups) is higher than under 95 kPa (1.55, 0.96, 0.45, and 0.21), indicating that combustion efficiency of combustible ejections decreases with reduction in ambient pressure from 95 kPa to 20 kPa.
claims.quote: The ratios of CO/CO2 are 1.55, 0.96, 0.45, and 0.21, for the four groups of tests under 95 kPa, respectively, and are lower than the ratios of 2.05, 1.99, 1.87, and 2.55 under 20 kPa... This indicates that the combustion efficiency of combustible ejections decreases with the reduction in the external pressure from 95 kPa to 20 kPa.

claims.claim: LFP tests did not flame at 0% and 30% SOCs, while LCO tests flamed at all SOCs, consistent with LFP cells having higher thermal stability compared to LCO cells.
claims.quote: LFP tests did not flame at 0% and 30% SOCs, while LCO tests flamed at all SOCs (Figure 5b). This is consistent with LFP cells having higher thermal stability compared to LCO cells.

claims.claim: OC was more abundant during cell venting, while EC and PO4 3− were more abundant when flaming combustion occurred.
claims.quote: While OC was more abundant during cell venting, EC and PO4 3− were more abundant when flaming combustion occurred.

claims.claim: For LFP tests, the average EC fraction increased from 0–7% for 0–50% SOC to 25–29% for 75% SOC.
claims.quote: The average EC fraction increased from 0–7% for 0–50% SOC to 25–29% for 75% SOC.

claims.claim: Ignition of the emissions stream, rather than cell SOC, is mostly responsible for the general composition of emitted PM2.5 particles.
claims.quote: This indicates that ignition of the emissions stream, rather than cell SOC, is mostly responsible for the general composition of emitted PM2.5 particles.

claims.claim: For LFP tests at 50% and 75% SOCs dominated by flaming combustion, the PO4 3− fraction dominated with 34–44% of PM2.5 mass, while OM fractions were much lower at 1–20%, compared to high OM at 0% and 30% SOC.
claims.quote: Compared to the 0% and 30% SOC cases, the OM fractions were much lower (1–20%), while the PO4 3− fraction, practically absent in the 0% and 30% SOC cases, dominated with 34–44% of PM2.5 mass.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway is characterised by rapid self-heating starting at around 150–220 °C and increasing to about 1000 °C.
claims.quote: thermal runaway is characterised by rapid self-heating starting at around 150–220 ◦C and increasing to about 1000 ◦C

claims.claim: The first step towards thermal runaway during overheating is the breakdown of the SEI layer on the graphite anode, which occurs at about 90–120 °C.
claims.quote: the first step towards TR is commonly the breakdown of the SEI layer on the graphite anode, which occurs at about 90–120 ◦C

claims.claim: When the separator melts at temperatures in the range of 135–165 °C, electrical contact between the anode and cathode can result in an internal short circuit and self-discharge.
claims.quote: When the separator melts at temperatures in the range of 135–165 ◦C, the electrical contact between the anode and cathode can result in an ISC and, thereby, self-discharge.

claims.claim: As temperature reaches 200 °C and higher, all components of the electrolyte start to decompose exothermically, forming CO2, HF, hydrocarbons, and various fluorinated hydrocarbon gases.
claims.quote: As the temperature reaches 200 ◦C and higher, all components of the electrolyte start to decompose exothermically, upon which CO2, hydrogen fluoride (HF), hydrocarbons, and various fluorinated hydrocarbon gases are formed.

claims.claim: The cathode begins to decompose and release oxygen in the temperature range of 150–310 °C.
claims.quote: As the temperature increases further in the range of 150–310 ◦C, the cathode begins to decompose and release oxygen.

claims.claim: The critical surface temperatures at the time of thermal runaway (Tf-TR) for four 50 Ah battery types are: LFP (346°C) > NIBs (292°C) > NCM523 (290°C) > NCM622 (281°C), with LFP exhibiting the highest thermal runaway critical temperature.
claims.quote: The critical temperature for thermal runaway (Tf-TR) of the batteries near the heating plate are as follows: LiFePO4 (LFP) battery: 346 °C, Sodium-ion battery (NIBs): 292 °C, NCM523 battery: 290 °C, NCM622 battery: 281 °C.

claims.claim: NIBs batteries have the lowest thermal runaway triggering energy (158 kJ) while LFP batteries have the highest (592.8 kJ), meaning NIBs are easiest and LFP is hardest to trigger into thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The heat input (Q) required to trigger thermal runaway was: LFP (592.8 kJ) > NCM622 (262 kJ) > NCM523 (274 kJ) > NIBs (158 kJ). NIBs batteries required the least energy to trigger thermal runaway, while LFP batteries required the most.

claims.claim: For NCM622 and NCM523 batteries, the opening of the safety valve and the onset of violent thermal runaway occur almost simultaneously, making it nearly impossible to use safety valve opening as an early warning indicator for thermal runaway.
claims.quote: for the two types of NCM lithium batteries, due to their higher energy density, the opening of the safety valve and the intense thermal runaway (sharp temperature rise) occur simultaneously. Therefore, it is almost impossible to use the opening of the safety valve as an early warning indicator for thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway propagation rates from largest to smallest are: NCM523 (3.375 mm/s) > NCM622 (2.7 mm/s) > NIBs (1.726 mm/s) > LFP (1.241 mm/s), with LFP exhibiting the lowest propagation rate due to lower thermal reactivity of LiFePO4.
claims.quote: The propagation rate for the sodium-ion battery is 1.726 mm/s, for the LFP battery is 1.241 mm/s, for the NCM523 battery is 3.375 mm/s, and for the NCM622 battery is 2.7 mm/s. The propagation rates from largest to smallest are: NCM523 > NCM622 > NIBs > LFP. The LFP battery exhibits the lowest thermal runaway propagation rate, attributed to the lower thermal reactivity of the LiFePO4 material.

claims.claim: NIBs have the lowest lower flammability limit (LFL = 3.39) among the four battery types tested, indicating the highest explosion risk, primarily due to a significant amount of propylene (C3H6) in the vented gas.
claims.quote: It was found that the sodium-ion battery had the lower flammability limit, with an LFL value of 3.98. This is primarily because the gases produced during the thermal runaway of the sodium-ion battery contain a significant amount of propylene (C3H6), which has the lower flammability limit. Consequently, the mixed gases from the sodium-ion battery exhibit the lower flammability limit, resulting in the highest explosion hazard.

claims.claim: NCM433 initiates thermal decomposition at 245°C, whereas NCM811 begins at 135°C, with higher nickel content correlating with lower thermal decomposition onset temperatures and greater oxygen release.
claims.quote: higher nickel content correlates with lower thermal decomposition onset temperatures and greater oxygen release. For example, NCM433 initiates decomposition at 245 ◦C, whereas NCM811 begins at 135 ◦C

claims.claim: For all NCM materials, singlet oxygen evolution initiates when SOC exceeds 80%, independent of electrode potential but dependent on lithium extraction levels.
claims.quote: For all NCM materials, singlet oxygen (1O2) evolution initiates when SOC exceeds 80%, independent of electrode potential but dependent on lithium extraction levels.

claims.claim: In NCM cathodes, 1% lattice oxygen loss causes up to 21% capacity fade.
claims.quote: Lattice oxygen loss not only drives gas evolution, but also severely degrades capacity, with 1% oxygen loss causing up to 21% capacity fade

claims.claim: Under overcharge conditions, LNMO cathodes maintain minimal gas evolution even at 5.0 V, while NCM cathodes generate substantial CO and CO2 below 4.8 V.
claims.quote: while NCM cathodes generate substantial CO and CO2 below 4.8 V, LNMO cathodes maintain minimal gas evolution, even at 5.0 V

claims.claim: Significant gas release from LFP cells initiates at approximately 120% SOC during overcharge, with substantial gas evolution and a temperature rise to approximately 140°C occurring at 130% SOC, followed by HF generation during cooling.
claims.quote: Overcharge testing of LFP cells reveals gas evolution, including CO2, H2, C2H4, CO, and C2H2F, with significant gas release initiating at ~120% SOC (Figure 4b). At 130% SOC, substantial gas evolution occurs, accompanied by a temperature rise to ~140 ◦C, followed by HF generation during cooling

claims.claim: The LFP cathode STA-MS test shows no O2 release below 600°C, and the exothermic reaction occurring after 500°C correlates with CO2 generation likely caused by a chemical reaction between the cathode and its graphite coating.
claims.quote: the STA-MS test results for a single cathode indicate no O2 release below 600 ◦C. Additionally, the exothermic reaction occurring after 500 ◦C correlates with the generation of CO2, which is likely caused by a chemical reaction between the cathode and the graphite coating on its surface.

claims.claim: Above 250°C, significant HF, fluoroalkanes, and ethers are generated due to oxygen release from NCM cathodes, whereas below 250°C thermal failure species remain largely unchanged.
claims.quote: Below 250 ◦C, thermal failure species remain largely unchanged; however, above 250 ◦C, significant HF, fluoroalkanes (e.g., C2H5F), and ethers (e.g., dimethyl ether) are generated due to oxygen release from NCM cathodes.

claims.claim: The SEI decomposes exothermically at 90–120°C, with metastable components such as (CH2OCO2Li)2 decomposing to produce C2H4, CO2, and O2.
claims.quote: The SEI decomposes exothermically at 90–120 ◦C, with metastable components such as (CH2OCO2Li)2 decomposing to produce C2H4, CO2, and O2 via the reaction: 2(CH2OCO2Li)2 → 2Li2CO3 + 2C2H4 + 2CO2 + O2

claims.claim: Adding vinylene carbonate (VC) as a SEI-forming additive reduces H2 evolution to 13% of its original level and reduces ethylene release by 60%, but generates CO2 as a byproduct.
claims.quote: adding vinylene carbonate (VC) forms a protective SEI layer, reducing H2 evolution to 13% of its original level. VC additives also reduce ethylene release by 60% but generate CO2 as a byproduct

claims.claim: In 1.0M LiPF6 EA/FEC electrolyte, gas evolution at −20°C far exceeds that at 45°C due to the inability of plated Li to passivate, resulting in vigorous reactions between EA and Li to form H2 and alkanes.
claims.quote: In 1.0M LiPF6 EA/FEC electrolyte, gas evolution at −20 ◦C far exceeds that at 45 ◦C due to the inability of plated Li to passivate, resulting in vigorous reactions between EA and Li to form H2 and alkanes

claims.claim: Flammable CH4 and CO account for 93% of the total gas generated in lithium metal batteries, which also produce CH4, CO, CO2, O2, C2H6, H2, C2H4, C3H6, C3H8, C4H8, and C4H10.
claims.quote: Lithium metal batteries generate significant gases, including CH4, CO, CO2, O2, C2H6, H2, C2H4, C3H6, C3H8, C4H8, and C4H10, with flammable CH4 and CO accounting for 93% of the total

claims.claim: During thermal runaway, elevated temperatures above 80°C induce SEI decomposition, releasing heat and flammable gases such as H2, which subsequently form explosive mixtures with cathode-derived oxygen.
claims.quote: elevated temperatures (>80 ◦C) induce SEI decomposition, releasing heat and flammable gases (e.g., H2), which subsequently form explosive mixtures with cathode-derived oxygen

claims.claim: Silicon-based anodes exhibit accelerated gas generation during early thermal runaway due to exothermic Si-Li alloy reactions with enthalpy greater than 1500 J/g, where released H2 reacts with electrolyte decomposition products such as PF5 to form toxic gases including HPF6.
claims.quote: silicon-based anodes exhibit accelerated gas generation during early thermal runaway due to exothermic Si-Li alloy reactions (∆H > 1500 J/g), where released H2 reacts with electrolyte decomposition products (e.g., PF5) to form toxic gases (e.g., HPF6)

claims.claim: Pure LiPF6 decomposes at 107°C in dry environments but remains stable at 90°C; in the presence of trace water (300 ppm), decomposition occurs at 87°C.
claims.quote: pure LiPF6 decomposes at 107 ◦C in dry environments, but remains stable at 90 ◦C. In the presence of trace water (300 ppm), decomposition occurs at 87 ◦C

claims.claim: At 85°C, 52% of CO2 and 60% of CO originate from EC decomposition, and EC reduction dominates at the anode surface regardless of co-solvents.
claims.quote: At 85 ◦C, 52% of CO2 and 60% of CO originate from EC decomposition. EC reduction dominates at the anode surface, regardless of co-solvents (DMC or DEC)

claims.claim: High-specific-surface-area conductive carbon additives such as Super P reduce the onset temperature of thermal runaway by approximately 20°C.
claims.quote: The incorporation of such conductive agents reduces the onset temperature of thermal runaway by approximately 20 ◦C, compromises the chemical stability of the system, and promotes gas generation through accelerated decomposition reactions

claims.claim: Residual Li2CO3 on cathode surfaces decomposes at 3.8 V to generate highly reactive singlet oxygen (1O2), which is not released as molecular oxygen O2.
claims.quote: Li2CO3 decomposes at 3.8 V to generate highly reactive 1O2, which is not released as molecular oxygen (O2).

claims.claim: Using 18O isotopic labeling, it was demonstrated that the generation of CO2 and CO during the initial cycle is almost entirely attributed to the decomposition of Li2CO3, with negligible contribution from electrolyte decomposition at the cathode.
claims.quote: Using 18O isotopic labeling, it was demonstrated that the generation of CO2 and CO during the initial cycle is almost entirely attributed to the decomposition of Li2CO3, with negligible contribution from electrolyte decomposition at the cathode

claims.claim: During storage at 60°C, gas generation in cathode electrode formulations predominantly occurs at conductive carbon surfaces rather than on cathode active materials such as LNMO, and the specific surface area of the carbon correlates positively with gas production.
claims.quote: during storage at 60 ◦C, gas generation predominantly occurs at conductive carbon surfaces rather than on cathode active materials (e.g., LNMO), and the specific surface area of the carbon correlates positively with gas production.

claims.claim: In LiCoO2-based cells tested at 90°C, significant gas evolution primarily comprising CO2, CO, and C2H4 occurs when the state of charge exceeds approximately 80% during charging.
claims.quote: Testing of LiCoO2-based cells at 90 ◦C revealed significant gas evolution (primarily CO2, CO, and C2H4) when the state of charge (SOC) exceeds ~80% during charging

claims.claim: β-Li3PS4 sulfide solid electrolyte cells with indium anodes show SO2 release closely linked to O2 evolution, while Li6PS5Cl SSEs show no H2S or SO2 generation during normal cycling at 45°C.
claims.quote: SO2 release was observed in cells with indium anodes, closely linked to O2 evolution... In contrast, Li6PS5Cl SSEs show no H2S or SO2 generation.

claims.claim: In LiCoO2|PEO-LiTFSI|Li cells, noticeable H2 generation occurs at 4.2 V, while a variety of gases including H2, CH4, H2O, C2H2, C2H4, CO, C2H6, HCHO, O2, CH3OH, CO2, CH3CHO, CH3CH2OH, and HCOOH are released at 4.5 V.
claims.quote: in LiCoO2|PEO-LiTFSI|Li cells, noticeable H2 generation occurs at 4.2 V, while a variety of gases, including H2, CH4, H2O, C2H2, C2H4, CO, C2H6, HCHO, O2, CH3OH, CO2, CH3CHO, CH3CH2OH, and HCOOH, are released at 4.5 V.

claims.claim: Coating PEO with LATP enhances its stability, suppressing gas evolution even at 4.6 V.
claims.quote: Coating PEO with LATP enhances its stability, suppressing gas evolution even at 4.6 V

claims.claim: Among the three major cathode materials, LFP exhibits the highest gas evolution onset temperature and the lowest gas volume, indicating superior safety.
claims.quote: Among the three cathode materials, LFP exhibits the highest gas evolution onset temperature and the lowest gas volume, underscoring its superior safety

claims.claim: LMO cells generate approximately twice the CO volume of NCM counterparts during comparative thermal runaway studies.
claims.quote: Comparative thermal runaway studies show that LMO cells generate approximately twice the CO volume of NCM counterparts

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of a prismatic 51 Ah NMC622 cell at 100% SOC, the cells lost approximately 60% of their mass as vent gases and particles.
claims.quote: During TR, the cells lost approximately 60% of their mass as vent gases and particles.

claims.claim: The presence of re-solidified copper droplets in ejected particles indicates that internal cell temperatures locally exceeded 1085 °C (the melting temperature of copper) during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The latter require inside cell TR temperatures that, at least locally, exceed 1085 ◦C, the melting temperature of copper. This finding well aligns with measured vent gas temperatures above 1100 ◦C.

claims.claim: Based on intact separator foil pieces and molten copper droplets, particle temperatures during ejection from the cell ranged from approximately 200 °C to over 1100 °C.
claims.quote: Based on intact separator foil pieces and molten copper droplets, particle temperatures during their ejection from the cell varied from about 200 ◦C to over 1100 ◦C.

claims.claim: Simultaneous thermal analysis of ejected NMC622 abuse particles revealed graphite combustion as the main exothermic event between 500–750 °C, causing a mass loss of approximately 10–30% depending on particle size.
claims.quote: Simultaneous thermal analysis of the seven smallest size fractions revealed graphite combustion as the main exothermic event between 500–750 ◦C, causing a mass loss of ~10–30% depending on particle size.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway heat release for a 51 Ah NMC622 prismatic cell at 100% SOC was estimated at approximately 1 MJ, corresponding to approximately 1.5 times the electrical energy stored in the cell.
claims.quote: Based on an energy balance that considers the increase in internal energy of all setup components, the TR heat release can be estimated to be about 1 MJ, which correlates to approximately 1.5 times the electrical energy stored in the cell at 100% SOC.

claims.claim: For NMC622 abuse particles, sphericity, symmetry, and aspect ratio decrease for larger particles, indicating that larger particles are more non-spherical.
claims.quote: Particles are clearly non-spherical, with sphericity, symmetry, and aspect ratio decreasing for larger particles.

claims.claim: Particle reactivity (mass loss during STA) was non-linearly size dependent, with size classes 0–32 µm and 125–250 µm containing the highest proportion of graphite at almost 30% of total mass.
claims.quote: Size classes 0–32 µm and 125–250 µm show the highest mass loss and thus contain the highest proportion of graphite. Reactivity is non-linearly size dependent.

claims.claim: A total of 168 metallic conductive particles with a maximum Feret diameter above 10 mm were identified in a single particle sample from a 51 Ah NMC622 prismatic cell thermal runaway, with the largest particle reaching 51.4 mm.
claims.quote: A total of 168 metallic particles with a maximum Feret diameter above 10 mm could be identified in sample #3, with the largest particle reaching 51.4 mm.

claims.claim: The reaction onset temperature for ejected NMC622 abuse particles in an oxidative atmosphere was approximately 500–600 °C, with peak heat release between 600–750 °C.
claims.quote: The reaction onset of the latter was determined to be around 500–600 ◦C in this study, with peak heat release between 600–750 ◦C.

claims.claim: Ninety-five percent of ejected abuse particles from a prismatic NMC622 cell had a size below 1 mm, while 60% of the particles were smaller than 63 µm by mass-weighted sieve analysis.
claims.quote: Ninety-five percent of the particles have a size below 1 mm, while 60% of the particles are smaller than 63 µm.

claims.claim: Of the three cell chemistries tested, LFP releases the least normalized vent gas at 0.02 mol/Ah, followed by NVPF at 0.05 mol/Ah, and NMC at the highest at 0.07 mol/Ah, when triggered by two-sided overtemperature.
claims.quote: Of the three cell chemistries, LFP releases the least amount of vent gas at 0.02 mol/Ah (41% H2, 27% CO2, 8% CO), followed by NVPF at 0.05 mol/Ah (42% CO2, 17% electrolyte solvent, 15% H2 and 10% CO) and NMC at 0.07 mol/Ah (36% CO, 24% CO2, 19% H2).

claims.claim: The maximum vent gas temperature increases from NVPF (265 °C) to LFP (446 °C) to NMC (1050 °C) under two-sided overtemperature triggering.
claims.quote: The maximum vent gas temperature increases from NVPF (265 ◦C) to LFP (446 ◦C) and NMC (1050 ◦C).

claims.claim: Overcharge trigger produces the highest vent gas volume for NVPF cells at 1.23 mol (0.07 mol/Ah), and also results in the highest H2 concentration at 23% among all trigger methods tested.
claims.quote: The overcharge trigger produces the most amount of vent gas, 1.23 mol... this trigger exhibits the highest recorded H2 (23%) concentration, resulting from the side reactions, thereby increasing the explosion risk of the mixture.

claims.claim: The external short-circuit trigger produces the lowest amount of vent gas for NVPF cells at 0.14 mol, with the lowest peak vent gas temperature of 94 °C and cell surface temperature of 106 °C among all triggers tested.
claims.quote: The lowest amount of vent gas is measured for the externally shorted-circuited cell, at 0.14 mol. The peak vent gas and cell surface temperatures are the lowest recorded, at 94 ◦C and 106 ◦C, respectively.

claims.claim: The flammability window of NVPF vent gas mixture is between 7% and 28%, which is narrower than those of NMC (7% to 46%) and LFP (6% to 45%), indicating reduced explosion risk for NVPF compared to Li-ion cells.
claims.quote: This window is between 7% and 28% for the vent gas mixture of the NVPF cell. The Li-ion cells have a calculated flammability window of 7% to 46% and 6% to 45% for the NMC and LFP chemistry, respectively.

claims.claim: Increasing ventilation rate decreases gas concentration and shortens the duration of battery pack exposure to explosion risk during thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: increasing ventilation rate can decrease the gas concentration and shorten the duration of battery pack under explosion

claims.claim: Increasing cell distance reduces thermal runaway propagation speed but inhibits rapid dispersion of venting gases, causing prolonged explosion risk exposure.
claims.quote: Despite of reducing TR propagation speeds, the increase of cell distance can inhibit the rapid dispersion of venting gases, causing battery pack exposed to a prolonged explosion risk.

claims.claim: Void volume has limited effect on the duration of battery pack exposure to explosion risk during thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: a limited effect of void volume is evident for that

claims.claim: When the battery pack has no active ventilation, vented gases achieve a saturation condition and gas concentrations remain essentially constant as thermal runaway propagates through successive cells.
claims.quote: for the battery pack without active ventilation, the concentrations of H2, CO, CO2 and CH4 initially reached 0.050 g⋅L−1, 0.045 g⋅L−1, 0.038 g⋅L−1 and 0.017 g⋅L−1 when the first cell went into TR. As the safety valve of the second cell opened, the gas concentrations continued to rise, until the void of battery pack was filled with the ejected gases. In this case, the ejected gases could ultimately achieve a saturation condition that they flowed straight out after being ejected, rather than accumulating in the convergence plenum.

claims.claim: Among Z-type, U-type, and I-type air-cooling battery pack flow channel layouts, the anti-explosion performance order is Z-type > U-type > I-type, with dimensionless time indices of 0.529, 0.98, and 0.99 respectively.
claims.quote: The time index τ were calculated as 0.529, 0.98 and 0.99 for the Z-type, U-type and I-type ACBP, respectively, which is the evidence of a better safety performance for Z-type compared with other designs.

claims.claim: NMC-based cells emit higher volumes of hazardous gases and propagate thermal runaway more readily than LFP cells, indicating chemistry-dependent risk profiles.
claims.quote: comparative experiments reveal that NMC-based cells emit higher volumes of hazardous gases and propagate TR more readily than LFP cells, indicating chemistry-dependent risk profiles

claims.claim: Using conductive nails in nail penetration tests causes greater temperature spikes than non-conductive nails.
claims.quote: Abaza et al. [108], for example, found that using conductive nails in NP tests causes greater temperature spikes than non-conductive ones.

claims.claim: A study comparing various thermal runaway initiation methods found different quantities and concentrations of emitted gases even for the same cell chemistry.
claims.quote: A study comparing various TR initiation methods found different quantities and concentrations of emitted gases even for the same cell chemistry

claims.claim: LiPF6 decomposes at relatively low temperatures (around 150–200 °C), releasing highly toxic and corrosive by-products such as hydrofluoric acid.
claims.quote: The most commonly used lithium salt, lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6), offers good ionic conductivity, but it decomposes at relatively low temperatures (around 150–200 °C), releasing highly toxic and corrosive by-products such as hydrofluoric acid.

claims.claim: Total heat generation increases non-linearly with SOC in NCA-based Li-ion cells under accelerating rate calorimetry, while the onset temperature of thermal runaway decreases with increasing SOC.
claims.quote: Figure 8 presents the results of the Accelerating Rate Calorimetry (ARC) test for NCA-based Li-ion cells, demonstrating that total heat generation increases non-linearly, while the onset temperature of TR decreases with SOC.

claims.claim: For NMC prismatic cells at 100% SOC, those tested in an open setup reached peak temperatures between 665 °C and 725 °C, while those tested in a closed N2-chamber exhibited higher values ranging from 838 °C to 857 °C, suggesting more constrained heat dissipation in closed chambers.
claims.quote: For NMC prismatic cells at 100% SOC, the ones tested in an open setup reached peak temperatures between 665 °C and 725 °C, while those tested inside a closed N2-chamber exhibited much higher values, ranging from 838 °C to 857 °C. This suggests that heat dissipation is more constrained in a closed chamber, leading to higher localized temperatures.

claims.claim: In nitrogen-filled chambers during nail penetration tests, the battery reached a maximum temperature of 47 °C, compared to 122 °C in an air-filled chamber, an 88.7% increase, demonstrating the suppressive influence of inert gas on combustion-driven temperature rise.
claims.quote: Diekmann et al. [115] reported that in a nitrogen-filled chamber, the battery only reached a maximum temperature of 47 °C, compared to 122 °C in an air-filled chamber, an 88.7% increase. This finding underscores the suppressive influence of inert gases on combustion-driven temperature rise.

claims.claim: Air environments during NP tests resulted in higher CO2 and lower H2 concentrations, while N2 environments produced the opposite trend.
claims.quote: the concentrations of H2 and CO2 can differ in tests conducted in air or inert environments: air environments resulted in higher CO2 and lower H2 concentrations, while N2 environments produced the opposite trend

claims.claim: LFP cathodes at 100% SOC exhibit an onset of heat release between 180 °C and 250 °C, with peak heating occurring between 210 °C and 360 °C.
claims.quote: LFP cathodes at 100% SOC exhibit an onset of heat release between 180 °C and 250 °C, with peak heating occurring between 210 °C and 360 ◦C

claims.claim: Increased humidity within a N2 environment promoted condensation of vapor-phase products on chamber surfaces, reducing the concentration of measurable gaseous species during nail penetration tests.
claims.quote: Their results showed that increased humidity within a N2 environment promoted condensation of vapour-phase products on chamber surfaces, reducing the concentration of measurable gaseous species

claims.claim: LFP cells did not undergo failure during nail penetration tests and showed no thermal runaway, fire, or visible failure indicators such as smoke, with surface temperatures remaining between 19 °C and 46 °C.
claims.quote: Howard et al. [116] reported that during NP of LFP prismatic and pouch cells, no TR, fire, or visible failure indicators such as smoke were observed. Surface temperatures for these LFP cells remained low, ranging from 19 °C to 46 °C.

claims.claim: NMC cells reached a maximum temperature ranging from 1010 °C to 1038 °C under NP-induced thermal runaway, in contrast to LFP cells that showed no thermal runaway.
claims.quote: they found that the NMC cells reached a maximum temperature ranging from 1010 °C to 1038 °C under NP-induced TR

claims.claim: A penetration speed of 1 mm/s, much lower than the 80 mm/s suggested in SAE standards, could significantly lower standard deviations in temperature and voltage profiles and allow more stable and consistent triggering of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Diekmann et al. [115] examined the influence of penetration speed (e.g., 1 mm/s, 5 mm/s, 10 mm/s, 40 mm/s, and 80 mm/s) and found that using a penetration speed of 1 mm/s, much lower than the 80 mm/s suggested in SAE standards, could significantly lower standard deviations in temperature and voltage profiles. This slow penetration rate was found to be able to allow for more stable and consistent triggering of TR

claims.claim: Cells at 50% SOC exhibited only swelling and gas release but did not enter thermal runaway during nail penetration tests of LCO pouch cells in an open environment, while cells at 100% SOC experienced thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Diaz et al. [113] performed NP on LCO pouch cells in an open environment, and they found that only cells with 100% SOC experienced TR during NP tests, while the cells at 50% SOC exhibited only swelling and gas release but did not enter runaway.

claims.claim: Carbon monoxide concentration during thermal runaway is directly correlated to the SOC of the battery.
claims.quote: carbon monoxide has been identified as one of the primary constituents, with its concentration directly correlated to the SOC of the battery

claims.claim: LFP batteries emit significantly higher amounts of CO during thermal runaway—up to an order of magnitude greater than LCO and NMC batteries.
claims.quote: lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries emit significantly higher amounts–up to an order of magnitude greater than LCO and NMC batteries

claims.claim: Hard-case prismatic cells could withstand a higher internal pressure (855 kPa) before venting compared to pouch cells (411 kPa) during NP tests conducted in an inert atmosphere.
claims.quote: hard-case prismatic cells could withstand a higher internal pressure (i.e., 855 kPa) before venting, compared to pouch cells (411 kPa). As the tests were conducted in an inert atmosphere, no fires were reported.

claims.claim: With increasing SOC in NMC prismatic cells, CO concentrations became dominant over CO2, whereas H2 concentrations showed a more gradual increase with SOC, almost evening out with CO2 concentration at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: With increasing SOC, CO concentrations became dominant over CO2, whereas H2 concentrations showed a more gradual increase with SOC, almost evening out with the CO2 concentration at 100% SOC

claims.claim: The total gas generation per unit capacity for NMC prismatic cells across studies with cell capacities ranging from 60 to 157 Ah ranged from 1.77 to 2.06 L/Ah, suggesting a correlation between cell capacity and gas evolution.
claims.quote: According to these studies, the total gas generation per unit capacity ranged from 1.77 to 2.06 L/Ah, suggesting a correlation between cell capacity and gas evolution.

claims.claim: Moisture in the atmosphere can react with fluorinated decomposition products (e.g., PF5 and POF3) to form hydrofluoric acid (HF), further increasing the corrosivity and toxicity of the emission plume during thermal abuse tests.
claims.quote: moisture in the atmosphere can react with fluorinated decomposition products (e.g., PF5 and POF3) to form hydrofluoric acid (HF), further increasing the corrosivity and toxicity of the emission plume.

claims.claim: All lithium ion cells tested vented gas and smoke and self-ignited to produce a flame when external heat was applied.
claims.quote: When external heat was applied, all the cells tested vented gas and smoke and self-ignited to produce a flame.

claims.claim: Aluminium was found in greater proportion in solid residues compared to smoke particulate, attributed to larger globules of molten aluminium ejecting from the cell and settling rapidly rather than being carried in the smoke.
claims.quote: it is clear that in many of these samples particularly in cells C, D and H, the proportion of aluminium observed in the solid residue is greater than that in the smoke particulate. This is most likely due to the larger sized globules of molten aluminium ejected which will either condense or rapidly react in air to form alumina compounds.

claims.claim: Copper is less likely than aluminium to be released from a failing lithium ion cell in smoke due to its higher melting point (1083 °C compared to 660 °C for aluminium).
claims.quote: Copper, with its much higher melting point (1083 1C as compared to 660 1C for aluminium) is less likely to have been released from the cell in this manner.

claims.claim: In failure events involving multiple cells that generate higher temperatures, more significant levels of copper may be seen in smoke and residues compared to single-cell failure events.
claims.quote: It may be that in failure events involving multiple cells, where which generate higher temperatures, that more significant levels of copper are seen in the smoke and in the residues.

claims.claim: The proportion of nickel in smoke particulate was slightly higher than in the near-field residues across all cells tested.
claims.quote: the proportion of nickel in the smoke particulate seems slightly higher than in the residues.

claims.claim: NMC nail penetration resulted in the highest PM2.5 emission rate of 551 g/h and total PN emission rate of 2.1E+17 particles/h (or 1.9E07 part./cm3) among all five tests.
claims.quote: This module resulted in the highest emission rates of 551 g/h of PM2.5 and 2.1E þ 17 particles/h (or 1.9E07 part./cm3) of total PN.

claims.claim: LFP modules subjected to nail penetration yielded relatively less emissions because thermal runaway propagation was not observed.
claims.quote: LFP modules subjected to nail penetration yielded relatively less emissions as propagation was not observed.

claims.claim: During LFP overcharge tests, HF peak concentration reached 177 ppm and remained above the NIOSH IDLH level of 30 ppm for several minutes under well-ventilated conditions.
claims.quote: the HF peak of 177 ppm is of the greatest concern. National Institute for Occupational Safety & Health (NIOSH) defines the level of HF that is immediately dangerous to life and health (IDLH) as 30 ppm, and the data show the measured concentration remaining above this level for several minutes even under these well-ventilated conditions.

claims.claim: No HF was observed during the NMC nail penetration test, likely because the electrolyte used an anion salt not containing fluoride.
claims.quote: Despite the high rate of electrolyte combustion, there was no HF observed in the NMC test at all, likely because of the use of an anion salt not containing fluoride.

claims.claim: NMC nail penetration test produced a peak CO2 value more than 20 times that observed in LFP overcharge tests, indicating a much larger mass of electrolyte combusted per second.
claims.quote: NMC nail penetration test resulted in a peak CO2 value that was more than 20 times that observed in LFP overcharge tests, indicating a much larger mass of electrolyte combusted per second.

claims.claim: The vent gas volume and combustion energy increased linearly with SOC for both LiCoO2 pouch cells (3.7 V, 4.8 Ah) and cylindrical cells (3.7 V, 2.6 Ah).
claims.quote: The vent gas volume and combustion energy increased linearly with SOC for the pouch and cylindrical cells.

claims.claim: Pouch cells heated at 20°C/min released an average of 14.1% more vent gas and had an average of 34.4% greater combustion energy compared to pouch cells heated at 5°C/min, across tested SOCs.
claims.quote: When comparing cells heated at 20°C/min to cells heated at 5°C/min, across the tested SOCs, the pouch cells released an average of 14.1% more vent gas and had an average of 34.4% greater combustion energy

claims.claim: Cylindrical cells heated at 20°C/min released an average of 7.2% more vent gas and had an average of 13.0% greater combustion energy compared to cylindrical cells heated at 5°C/min, across tested SOCs.
claims.quote: the cylindrical released an average of 7.2% more vent gas and had an average of 13.0% greater combustion energy

claims.claim: Cells heated at slower rates have less electrolyte remaining once thermal runaway occurs, which reduces the amount of vent gas and combustion energy compared to cells heated at faster rates, because slower heating allows more time for electrolyte to boil and vent before thermal runaway.
claims.quote: This is because slower heating rates allow more time for the electrolyte inside of the cells to boil and vent than with cells heated at faster heating rates. Therefore, cells heated at slower rates have less electrolyte remaining in the cells once thermal runaway actually occurs which reduces the amount of vent gas and combustion energy over the same cells tested at faster heating rates.

claims.claim: At 30% SOC, the percent change in combustion energy between heating rates of 5°C/min and 20°C/min was 129% for pouch cells and 64% for cylindrical cells, representing the maximum percent change across all tested SOCs.
claims.quote: The maximum percent change in combustion energy between a pouch cell heated at 5°C/min and 20°C/min was 129% and occurred in cells charged to 30% SOC... The maximum percent change in combustion energy between a cylindrical cell heated at 5°C/min and 20°C/min was 64% and occurred in cells charged to 30% SOC

claims.claim: The 5%vol Halon 1301 knockdown concentration and the sustained 3%vol Halon 1301 in a Class C cargo compartment may not be sufficient to inert lithium-ion battery vent gas and air mixtures, and 8.59%vol Halon 1301 is required to render all ratios of Li-Ion premixed battery vent gas in air inert.
claims.quote: the 5%vol Halon 1301 knockdown concentration and the sustained 3%vol Halon 1301 in a Class C cargo compartment may not be sufficient at inerting lithium-ion battery vent gas and air mixtures... Testing suggests that 8.59 %vol Halon 1301 is required to render all ratios of the Li-Ion pBVG in air inert

claims.claim: The average concentration of hydrogen by volume in vent gas across all SOCs was 24±4%vol for pouch cells and 12±4%vol for cylindrical cells.
claims.quote: The average concentration of hydrogen by volume in vent gas across all SOCs for the pouch and cylindrical cells were 24±4%vol and 12±4%vol, respectively.

claims.claim: The top three constituents as a percentage of vent gas by volume across all SOCs for pouch cells were carbon dioxide, hydrogen, and carbon monoxide, contributing on average 81±2%vol of the total gas mixture.
claims.quote: The top three constituents as a percentage of the vent gas by volume across all SOCs for the pouch and cylindrical cells were carbon dioxide, hydrogen, and carbon monoxide. On average, the three gases contribute to 81±2%vol and 54±12%vol of the total gas mixture for pouch cells and cylindrical cells, respectively.

claims.claim: For LiCoO2 pouch cells, the percentage of carbon dioxide by volume decreased while carbon monoxide by volume increased with increasing SOC.
claims.quote: Measurements show that the percentage of carbon dioxide by volume decreased while carbon monoxide by volume increased with increasing SOCs

claims.claim: A previous FAA study statistically determined that heating rates at or above 15°C/min were more likely to produce a violent thermal runaway reaction than cells heated at less than 15°C/min, with critical thresholds defined as a maximum cell case temperature above 250°C and release of over 0.5 L of vent gas.
claims.quote: the previously mentioned FAA study which tested 22 cylindrical cells at 30 % state of charge and statistically determined that heating rates at or above 15°C/min were more likely to produce a violent thermal runaway reaction than cells heated at less than 15°C/min. The critical thresholds for a violent thermal runaway reaction were determined to be a maximum cell case temperature above 250°C and the release of over 0.5 L of vent gas

claims.claim: Thermal runaway is an uncontrolled and self-propagating exothermic reaction within a lithium-ion battery that leads to rapid temperature increase, venting of combustible gases, and potential thermal events.
claims.quote: Thermal runaway is an uncontrolled and self-propagating exothermic reaction within the battery that leads to rapid increase in temperature, venting of combustible gases and potential thermal events [7].

claims.claim: The NCA battery vent gas mixture contains 10% CO2, 37.1% CO, 42.8% H2, 7.1% CH4, and 3% C2H4, while the LFP mixture contains 48.4% CO2, 29.5% CO, 5.6% H2, 7% CH4, and 0.5% C2H6.
claims.quote: Gas Mixtures CO2 CO H2 CH4 C2H4 C2H6 NCA 10 37.1 42.8 7.1 3 0 LFP 48.4 29.5 5.6 7 0 0.5

claims.claim: At room temperature, the breakdown voltage of the NCA gas mixture is higher than that of the LFP gas mixture for the majority of electrode separation distances, but at 300 °C the opposite is true.
claims.quote: In Figure 3.13, it can be seen that the breakdown voltage of NCA is higher than that of LFP for the majority of the separation distances at room temperature. Interestingly, the opposite is true for the two gas mixtures at 300 °C.

claims.claim: Increasing electrode separation distance increases the minimum breakdown voltage, regardless of the gas medium, because the increased volume of gas represents increased resistance.
claims.quote: increasing the distance between the electrodes increases the minimum breakdown voltage. This can be expected, as the increased distance results in an increased volume of gas which must be ionized... Therefore, it is reasonable to conclude that a higher voltage is required to produce a current between the electrodes.

claims.claim: Under fuel-rich conditions with NCA gas, the sustainability of the flame is driven by H radicals rather than OH radicals, indicating combustion can be sustained even where there is a low amount of oxygen present.
claims.quote: This presents a crucial conclusion: The sustainability of a flame resulting from ignition of the NCA gas mixture is not driven by OH, but rather by H. This indicates the combustion process can occur and be sustained even where there is a low amount of oxygen present. This has major implications in the consideration of design and safety protocols for battery safety.

claims.claim: The TR trigger temperature of LFP batteries (~184°C) is higher than that of NCM ternary batteries (~130–140°C), indicating better thermal stability for LFP.
claims.quote: The TR trigger temperature of LFP battery is about 180 °C, which is higher than that of the NCM ternary battery, which is around 130–140 °C.

claims.claim: Under standard conditions, the normalized gas production of NCM ternary batteries ranges from 1.8 to 2.8 L/Ah, while that of LFP batteries is significantly lower at only 0.569 L/Ah.
claims.quote: The gas production of the NCM battery is between 1.814–2.752 L/Ah after normalization, while the gas production of the LFP battery is significantly lower at 0.569 L/Ah.

claims.claim: In the gas production of LFP batteries during thermal runaway, H2 accounts for 50.82% while CO accounts for only 9.3%, resulting in a lower lower flammability limit (LFL) compared to NCM battery TR gas.
claims.quote: In the gas production of LFP batteries, H2 accounts for 50.82%, while CO only accounts for 9.3%... The TR formation gas of the LFP battery contains a higher amount of H2 compared to the NCM battery, resulting in a lower LFL due to the relatively low LFL of H2.

claims.claim: Based on gas production volume, the degree of harm from thermal runaway is ranked NCM905 > NCM811 > NCM622 > NCM523 > LFP, but based on gas flammability composition, the hazard ranking reverses to LFP > NCM811 > NCM622 > NCM523 > NCM905.
claims.quote: Based on battery gas production, the degree of harm caused by TR can be ranked as follows: NCM9 0.5 0.5 > NCM811 > NCM622 > NCM523 > LFP... from the perspective of thermal runaway gas composition, the hazard ranking is LFP > NCM811 > NCM622 > NCM523 > NCM9 0.5 0.5.

claims.claim: In NCM batteries, higher nickel content leads to a lower initial temperature of oxygen release and worse thermal stability, while the presence of Mn improves thermal stability.
claims.quote: In NCM batteries, Ni is the most unstable element, with higher nickel content leading to a lower initial temperature of oxygen release and worse thermal stability. The presence of Mn can improve thermal stability.

claims.claim: When the excess air coefficient is around 0.9, the combustion mode of venting gas in the battery pack channel changes from slow deflagration to fast deflagration, significantly increasing explosion risk.
claims.quote: When the excess air coefficient is around 0.9, the combustion mode changes. When the excess air coefficient is decreased to 0.8, the average flame velocity has exceeded the local sonic speed, and the combustion mode has changed from slow deflagration to fast deflagration, which significantly increases the risk of a combustion accident.

claims.claim: When the initial temperature of the venting gas mixture is 400 K, the flame velocity exceeds the local sonic speed and the combustion mode changes to fast deflagration, posing a high risk of explosion for the battery pack.
claims.quote: When the initial temperature is 400 K, the flame propagation shows a different variation compared with the other three initial temperatures. The flame velocity exceeds the local sonic speed, and the deflagration mode occurs for the combustion of the venting gas mixture. Therefore, if the venting gas of a lithium-ion battery mixes with the air and the temperature is higher than 400 K, there is a high risk of fast deflagration and even the explosion of the battery pack.

claims.claim: As initial pressure increases from 81 kPa to 174 kPa (at 329 K, equivalence ratio 1), maximum average flame velocity increases from 93.5 m/s to 151.5 m/s, and flame propagation time decreases.
claims.quote: The maximum average flame velocities are 93.5 m·s−1, 100 m·s−1, 138.2 m·s−1, and 151.5 m·s−1, corresponding to an initial pressure of 81 kPa, 101 kPa, 154 kPa, and 174 kPa. A similar trend is displayed with the propagation time. However, no deflagration appears, and the combustion mode is not changed.

claims.claim: As initial temperature increases from 300 K to 400 K (at 154 kPa, equivalence ratio 1), maximum average flame velocity increases from 109.2 m/s to 402.5 m/s, with propagation time decreasing by up to 60.6% relative to 300 K.
claims.quote: At the initial temperature of 300 K, 329 K, 360 K, and 400 K, the maximum average flame velocity is 109.2 m·s−1, 138.2 m·s−1, 264.7 m·s−1, and 402.5 m·s−1, respectively. The results imply a high sensitivity of the initial temperature to the flame velocity of the venting gas.

claims.claim: When the excess air coefficient is 0.8, the maximum combustion overpressure caused by the combustion wave (91.3 kPa) exceeds that caused by the ignition pressure wave (61.7 kPa), and the maximum flame speed reaches approximately 445 m/s.
claims.quote: The maximum velocity arrives at 445 m·s−1 when the excess air coefficient is 0.8. When the excess air coefficient is 0.8, the peak overpressure caused by the ignition pressure wave is 61.7 kPa, which is less than that caused by the combustion wave of 91.3 kPa.

claims.claim: For a 50 Ah LFP battery at 100% SOC, when the surface temperature reaches between 190–210 °C, the temperature increase rate accelerates to 37 °C/min and voltage decreases to zero instantly.
claims.quote: When the surface temperature of the battery reaches between 190–210 °C, the thermal regulation of the battery is activated, the temperature increase rate accelerates to 37 °C/min, and the voltage decreases to zero instantly.

claims.claim: The maximum battery surface temperature reached approximately 320 °C during thermal runaway of the 50 Ah LFP cell at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: Eventually, the battery temperature reaches a maximum of around 320 °C.

claims.claim: At 0% SOC, the LFP battery valve opened but no major secondary eruption occurred, whereas a second eruption occurred at both 50% and 100% SOC.
claims.quote: At 0% SOC, the battery valve was opened and there was no major secondary eruption. A second eruption occurred at 50% and 100% SOC, and the manifested traits are largely constant.

claims.claim: The severity of thermal runaway increased with increasing SOC, with maximum temperatures following the order 100% TMAX > 50% TMAX > 0% TMAX for the 50 Ah LFP battery.
claims.quote: The severity of the thermal runaway increased with increasing SOC, as shown by the graphic in Figure 4, 100% TMAX > 50% TMAX > 0% TMAX.

claims.claim: H2 accounts for approximately 50% of the normalized gas volume produced by 50 Ah LFP batteries during thermal runaway, making it one of the most significant flammable gas constituents.
claims.quote: From the perspective of gas production, H2 accounts for a relatively high proportion of the gas generated by lithium iron phosphate batteries, approaching about 50%.

claims.claim: The safety valve of the LFP battery opens at approximately 160 °C, before thermal runaway occurs, as identified from the inflection point in the temperature rise rate curve.
claims.quote: A tangent with multiple stages of temperature rise rate can be found in the vicinity of 160 °C at the junctures... it can be seen from this angle that the lithium iron phosphate battery valve opens before thermal runaway.

claims.claim: LFP cells exhibited a maximum cell temperature of 305 °C during thermal runaway, compared to NCA cells which can reach maximum temperatures of 1075 °C.
claims.quote: charged NCA cells showed a drastic thermal runaway behavior. NCA cells can reach maximum temperatures of 1075 °C. In this study, LFP cells exhibited a less pronounced thermal runaway with maximum cell temperatures as high as 305 °C.

claims.claim: The mass loss rate of the 50 Ah LFP battery increased with SOC: 16% at 0% SOC, 17% at 50% SOC, and 18% at 100% SOC.

During the first valve opening stage (battery temperature 160–200 °C), only a small amount of gas and electrolyte is released, and the nozzle temperature exhibits a sharp decline because the ejected material is cooler than the surrounding air.
claims.quote: First eruption area: The battery's temperature ranges from 160 to 200 °C. At this point, a small amount of gas leaks out because the pressure needed to open the safety valve is greater than the gas production pressure inside the battery. The temperature of the nozzle exhibits a sharp decline at this point because the temperature of the material being ejected is lower than the surrounding air temperature.

claims.claim: The interior pressure of the sealed experimental chamber underwent its first abrupt increase, rising to 110 kPa/s, when the safety valve opened during the first eruption stage of the 100% SOC LFP battery.
claims.quote: the experimental chamber's interior pressure undergoes its first abrupt increase, rising to 110 kPa/s.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the normalized gas amount for NCM batteries ranges within 0.075–0.105 mol/Ah, while for LFP batteries it is only 0.025–0.035 mol/Ah, implying NCM batteries generate more than around 1–3 times the gases in moles compared to LFP batteries.
claims.quote: the normalized gas amount varies within 0.075–0.105 mol/Ah (1.68–2.35 L/Ah), consistent with 0.09 mol/Ah [18] and 0.08 mol/Ah [17] to a certain degree, while that for LFP batteries is only 0.025–0.035 mol/L (0.56–0.78 L/Ah), close to 0.03 mol/Ah, found in previous work [17], implying that NCM batteries generate more than around 1–3 times the gases in moles compared to LFP batteries.

claims.claim: The maximum gas releasing rate shows a strongly positive correlation with battery capacity for NCM batteries (ranging 0.04–0.31 mol/s) rather than with specific energy density, while LFP batteries show only a slight increase (0.02–0.06 mol/s).
claims.quote: the maximum gas releasing rate presents a strongly positive correlation with the capacity of NCM batteries (0.04–0.31 mol/s) and a slight increase for LFP batteries (0.02–0.06 mol/s)

claims.claim: For 117 Ah prismatic NCM811 batteries, a venting event occurring at elevated temperatures (160–250 °C) produces approximately double the maximum gas releasing rate compared to room temperature, for both 50% SOC (0.374 mol/s vs. 0.185 mol/s) and 100% SOC (0.481 mol/s vs. 0.219 mol/s).
claims.quote: A venting event occurring at elevated temperatures tends to erupt more violently and increase by double, compared to either 0.374 mol/s under 160–250 degrees Celsius with 0.185 mol/s at room temperature for 50% SOC or 0.481 mol/s under 160–250 degrees Celsius with 0.219 mol/s at room temperature for 100% SOC.

claims.claim: For NCM811 prismatic batteries and 280 Ah LFP prismatic batteries, total gas amount and maximum gas releasing rate both show a positive correlation with SOC; at 100% SOC the ejection is violent and of short duration, while at 0% and 50% SOC thermal runaway is not triggered when the safety valve first opens.
claims.quote: A positive correlation of total gas amount and maximum gas releasing rate with SOC was satisﬁed for both LFP and NCM811 batteries... For 100% SOC, the ejection is violent and of short duration, producing a large amount of gases over 0% SOC and 50% SOC. For 0% SOC and 50% SOC, thermal runaway is not triggered when the safety valve opens, leading to a long period to release generated gases and vaporize electrolytes.

claims.claim: At elevated ambient temperatures, gas production for NCM811 batteries grew around 10% and characteristic venting durations reduced to approximately half of those measured at normal temperatures, for both 50% SOC and 100% SOC cases.
claims.quote: The gas production of both the 50% SOC case and the 100% SOC case grew around 10% at elevated temperatures, and the characteristic durations reduced to half the results at normal temperatures.

claims.claim: For NCA cells subjected to thermal abuse at 100% SoC and 5°C/min, CO is the most abundant species released at maximum temperature, reaching 2.25 × 10^5 ppmv.
claims.quote: For NCA cells, CO is the most abundant species released (2.25 × 105 ppmv).

claims.claim: For LFP cells subjected to thermal abuse at 100% SoC and 5°C/min, CO is entirely converted to CO2, with CO concentration being negligible (nil ppm) and CO2 concentration at 1.65 × 10^5 ppmv, attributed to the higher stability of the LFP cathode material which remains stable up to 500°C.
claims.quote: For LFP cells, CO is entirely converted to CO2, with the CO concentration being negligible (nil ppm) and a CO2 concentration at 1.65 × 105 ppmv. This behavior is attributed to the higher stability of the LFP cathode material, which remains stable up to 500 ◦C [24], a temperature not reached even during TR (maximum cell surface temperature around 310 ◦C).

claims.claim: Regardless of cell chemistry (NCA, LTO, or LFP) at 100% SoC, the concentrations of HF and CO exceed the IDLH limits of 30 ppm and 1200 ppm respectively over a 30-minute period; in the worst case, HF emissions from LFP cells exceed the IDLH limit by an order of magnitude.
claims.quote: The data in Table 5 indicate that, regardless of the chemical composition, the concentration values exceed the IDLH values of 30 ppm for HF [31] and 1200 ppm for CO [30] over a 30 min period as set by NIOSH. In the worst case, the HF emissions from the LFP cell exceed the limit by an order of magnitude.

claims.claim: NCA cells reached a maximum temperature of 511 ± 96°C and pressure of 5.071 barg during thermal runaway, which is over 200°C higher and approximately 3.3 barg greater than LTO (381 ± 89°C, 1.802 barg) and LFP (406 ± 62°C, 2.202 barg) cells under identical thermal abuse conditions at 100% SoC.
claims.quote: The maximum temperatures and pressures reached at the peak were 511 ± 96 ◦C and 5.071 barg for NCA, 381 ± 89 ◦C and 1.802 barg for LTO and 406 ± 62 ◦C and 2.202 barg for LFP. The differences in maximum temperature and pressure are significant, with NCA cells reaching over 200 ◦C higher temperatures compared to LTO and LFP cells.

claims.claim: The liquid ejected from LFP cells during thermal abuse was identified as ethylene carbonate (EC), and its presence is attributed to the cell surface temperature not exceeding 350°C during the TR event, which prevented complete evaporation of the EC in the electrolyte solution.
claims.quote: The liquid ejected from the LFP was identified as ethylene carbonate (EC), the solvent with the highest boiling temperature among the carbonates in the electrolytic solution, at approximately 244 ◦C. The presence of this liquid is attributable to the fact that the surface temperature of the cell in question did not exceed 350 ◦C during the TR event. This prevented the evaporation of the entirety of the EC present in the solution.
